VWCE.name: Vanguard FTSE All-World UCITS ETF (dist)
VWCE.thesis: Same fund as VWCE but distributing — pays quarterly dividend instead of accumulating.
whyPick: Cashflow if you want to live off dividends later
Tax-friendly in some jurisdictions (NL, IE) — neutral in Germany
whyNot: Forces re-investment manually; in Germany Vorabpauschale already covers acc
Distributions trigger Abgeltungsteuer events vs deferral in acc — slight drag for accumulator

VWCE.name: SPDR MSCI ACWI IMI UCITS ETF
VWCE.thesis: Broader than VWCE — ACWI IMI includes small caps (~9,000 stocks vs VWCE's 3,700).
whyPick: Adds small-cap factor exposure for free (historically +1%/yr premium, with extra vol)
Cheaper TER 0.17% vs 0.22%
whyNot: Lower trading volume than VWCE → wider spreads in Xetra
Small-cap tilt can lag in mega-cap-led rallies (last 5 yrs)

VWCE.name: iShares MSCI ACWI UCITS ETF (acc)
VWCE.thesis: iShares' answer to VWCE on the MSCI index family — ~2,800 large/mid stocks across DM + EM.
whyPick: MSCI index family aligns with most institutional benchmarks → easier to compare returns
Marginal fee saving 0.20% vs 0.22%
whyNot: Smaller universe than VWCE (FTSE All-World is ~3,700 vs MSCI ACWI ~2,800)
No real reason to switch from VWCE just for 2bps

VWCE.name: Invesco FTSE All-World UCITS ETF (acc)
VWCE.thesis: Newer Invesco FTSE All-World — same index as VWCE but cheaper. Uses sampling / synthetic replication.
whyPick: Lowest fee in category (0.15%) — save ~€15/yr per €10k vs VWCE
Same FTSE All-World index → near-identical exposure
whyNot: Smaller AUM, less trading history → tracking error harder to verify
Switching costs (Abgeltungsteuer on VWCE gains) likely exceed the fee saving for 15+ years

SWDA.name: iShares Core MSCI World UCITS ETF (acc) — IWDA
SWDA.thesis: Same fund as SWDA, different listing (Amsterdam vs London). Pick by broker fee.
whyPick: Identical exposure — pick whichever listing your broker prices best
whyNot: No real choice to make — they're the same fund

SWDA.name: iShares Core MSCI World UCITS ETF (Xetra, EUNL)
SWDA.thesis: Same fund again, Xetra listing — typically tightest spreads for German retail brokers.
whyPick: EUR-denominated trading, tight Xetra spreads
whyNot: Identical to SWDA — switching is admin, not strategy

SWDA.name: Xtrackers MSCI World UCITS ETF 1C
SWDA.thesis: Xtrackers' MSCI World — same index, marginally cheaper, smaller AUM than iShares.
whyPick: 1bp cheaper
Provider diversification away from iShares
whyNot: 1bp saving is irrelevant at this size
iShares MSCI World is industry-standard liquidity

SWDA.name: SPDR MSCI USA Small Cap Value Weighted UCITS ETF
SWDA.thesis: Differentiation play: instead of duplicating SWDA, replace it with a US small-cap value factor tilt.
whyPick: Genuine diversification — small-cap value has low correlation to MSCI World mega-caps
Long-run premium ~2-3%/yr (Fama-French) if it persists
whyNot: Factor premia have underperformed for 15+ years — no guarantee they return
30% higher TER and US-only — concentrates currency risk further

BGF.name: Polar Capital Global Technology Fund
BGF.thesis: Long-running active global tech fund — broader than 'next gen', includes mature large-cap tech with concentrated picks.
whyPick: 0.55% cheaper TER — meaningful over 15 yrs
Longer track record (incepted 2001) through multiple tech cycles
whyNot: Less concentrated in next-gen themes — overlap with XDWT is higher than BGF
Soft-closed periodically; access can be broker-dependent

BGF.name: Allianz Global Artificial Intelligence
BGF.thesis: AI-themed active fund. Concentrated portfolio focused on the AI value chain (semis, infra, applications).
whyPick: Purer AI exposure than BGF's broader 'next-gen' mandate
Strong recent performance riding the AI cycle
whyNot: Even higher TER (~2.05%)
Theme-fund risk: if AI narrative breaks, drawdown is severe (-50-60%)

BGF.name: Pictet — Robotics R EUR
BGF.thesis: Thematic Pictet fund — automation, robotics, industrial AI. Different angle on 'tech' than BGF.
whyPick: Genuine diversification vs BGF (industrial/robotics, not consumer tech)
Pictet thematic franchise has strong process
whyNot: Highest fee in this peer set
Robotics theme has lagged broad tech materially since 2021

BGF.name: Xtrackers MSCI World IT (XDWT) — already held
BGF.thesis: Replace BGF with XDWT entirely (Path A from June review). Cheap passive tech instead of paying for active.
whyPick: Saves ~1.55%/yr — over 15 yrs that's ~25% of portfolio value compounded
Eliminates active-manager risk in a sector where most managers fail to beat the index
whyNot: Gives up the chance that BGF's manager is in the right tail
You already chose Path B (keep BGF + add XDWT) for the bigger tech bet — switching reverses that

XDWT.name: iShares S&P 500 Information Technology Sector UCITS ETF
XDWT.thesis: US-only tech sector — purer mega-cap tech exposure (Apple, MSFT, NVDA dominate).
whyPick: Cheapest tech ETF in category (0.15%)
Even higher beta to the AI cycle than XDWT
whyNot: 100% US — concentrates currency and country risk
Loses ASML/TSM-adjacent exposure that XDWT picks up

XDWT.name: Invesco EQQQ NASDAQ-100 UCITS ETF
XDWT.thesis: Top 100 non-financial Nasdaq stocks. Tech-heavy but includes consumer (Amazon, Tesla), comms (Meta, Google).
whyPick: Broader 'growth' exposure than pure IT sector
Most-traded tech-tilted ETF in Europe — deep liquidity
whyNot: Not pure tech — adds non-tech consumer/comm names
US-only, same currency concentration as IUIT

XDWT.name: Amundi MSCI World Information Technology UCITS ETF
XDWT.thesis: Same index as XDWT (MSCI World IT) — Amundi version. Genuinely interchangeable.
whyPick: Provider diversification away from Xtrackers/DB
whyNot: No exposure difference — pure provider swap

XDWT.name: iShares Digital Security UCITS ETF
XDWT.thesis: Cybersecurity narrow theme — alternative tech sleeve with structural growth tailwind from cyber attacks/regulation.
whyPick: Genuine diversification within tech — cyber spend grows independent of broad tech cycles
Themed but defensible secular thesis
whyNot: Narrow theme = wider drawdowns; cyber stocks crashed -50% in 2022
Higher TER, less liquid

A3DRHJ.name: iShares Core MSCI EM IMI UCITS ETF
A3DRHJ.thesis: Pure passive MSCI EM IMI — broadest EM exposure including small caps. The benchmark.
whyPick: 1.82%/yr cheaper — biggest fee saving in this set
~3,000 stocks incl. EM small-caps; broadest possible EM
Boring works in EM too — active EM has lagged index over 10-yr windows often
whyNot: Cap-weighted means heavy China + tech concentration (~30% China)
No protection from EM-specific governance / political risk via stock selection

A3DRHJ.name: Fidelity Emerging Markets Fund Y EUR (acc)
A3DRHJ.thesis: Active Fidelity EM mandate — strong long-term track, conviction picks across EM.
whyPick: Cheaper than A3DRHJ by 1% while still active
Long established Fidelity EM franchise (Nick Price era + successors)
whyNot: Still much more expensive than EIMI
Manager-style risk — concentrated in quality/growth EM, lags in value-EM rallies

A3DRHJ.name: HSBC MSCI Emerging Markets UCITS ETF
A3DRHJ.thesis: Cheapest broad EM ETF on the market — straight MSCI EM passive.
whyPick: Lowest TER in EM space (0.15%)
Pure passive — no manager risk
whyNot: No small caps (MSCI EM standard, not IMI)
Same active-vs-passive tradeoff as EIMI; pick one or the other
